perturbation.original_text: 6. Any VIOLATION or THREATENED VIOLATION of this AGREEMENT shall entitle the COMPANY to INJUNCTIVE RELIEF, together with ANY OTHER REMEDIES available to the COMPANY including MONETARY DAMAGES. In the event of ACTUAL VIOLATION of the AGREEMENT, the COMPANY shall receive, from RECIPIENT 100% of ALL INCOME which RECIPIENT has acquired form said BREACH of this AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. If any provision in this AGREEMENT is held, by a COURT of competent jurisdiction, to be invalid, void or unenforceable, the remaining provision shall nevertheless CONTINUE in FULL FORCE, without being impaired or invalidated IN ANY WAY. The failure of the COMPANY to enforce any provision of this AGREEMENT shall NOT be construed as a waiver of any such provision, nor prevent the COMPANY from enforcing such provision or any other provision of this AGREEMENT. 6. Any VIOLATION or THREATENED VIOLATION of this AGREEMENT shall entitle the COMPANY to INJUNCTIVE RELIEF.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the remedies available to the company upon violation of the agreement to the end of paragraph 7 and truncating it obscures the company's right to monetary damages and full income recovery derived from a breach. This makes it harder to enforce the full scope of remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law outlines specific remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, including injunctive relief, damages for actual loss, and unjust enrichment. By obscuring the availability of monetary damages and full income recovery, the modified text weakens the enforceability of the remedies available under Texas law for trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 134. TEXAS THEFT LIABILITY ACT CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 6. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS CHAPTER 134. TEXAS THEFT LIABILITY ACT Sec. 134.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Texas Theft Liability Act. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Sec. 134.002. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (1) "Person" means an individual, partnership, corporation, association, or other group, however organized. (2) "Theft" means unlawfully appropriating property or unlawfully obtaining services as described by Section 31.03 , 31.04 , 31.06 , 31.07 , 31.11 , 31.12 , 31.13 , or 31.14 , Penal Code. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Amended by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 858, Sec. 4, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Amended by: Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 2, eff. September 1, 2013. Sec. 134.003. LIABILITY. (a) A person who commits theft is liable for the damages resulting from the theft. (b) A parent or other person who has the duty of control and reasonable discipline of a child is liable for theft committed by the child. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Sec. 134.004. SUIT. A suit under this chapter may be brought in the county where the theft occurred or in the county where the defendant resides. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989. Sec. 134.005. RECOVERY. (a) In a suit under this chapter, a person who has sustained damages resulting from theft may recover: (1) under Section 134.003 (a), from a person who commits theft, the amount of actual damages found by the trier of fact and, in addition to actual damages, damages awarded by the trier of fact in a sum not to exceed $1,000; or (2) from a parent or other person who has the duty of control and reasonable discipline of a child, for an action brought under Section 134.003 (b), the amount of actual damages found by the trier of fact, not to exceed $5,000. (b) Each person who prevails in a suit under this chapter shall be awarded court costs and reasonable and necessary attorney's fees. Added by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 2, Sec. 4.05(a), eff. Aug. 28, 1989.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states the company is entitled to "ANY OTHER REMEDIES available to the COMPANY including MONETARY DAMAGES" and "100% of ALL INCOME which RECIPIENT has acquired form said BREACH." The changed text removes the specific mention of monetary damages and full income recovery, weakening the company's ability to pursue these remedies as readily. While the changed text mentions injunctive relief, it obscures the clarity of monetary damages and income recovery. The Law Explanation indicates that this contradicts Texas law by weakening the enforceability of remedies for trade secret misappropriation, remedies that include damages for actual loss, and unjust enrichment. Scraped Law Snippet 1, under Section 134.005, allows a person who has sustained damages resulting from theft to recover "the amount of actual damages found by the trier of fact and, in addition to actual damages, damages awarded by the trier of fact in a sum not to exceed $1,000." The removal of clearly stated compensation in the original text hinders the process of recovering losses caused by theft. This corresponds to the Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction because the remedies that were stated in the original text were moved and truncated in a way that obscures them, affecting its enforceability and clarity.

perturbation.original_text: 2. All CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL, which RECIPIENT shall use, view, receive or come in contact with, shall be and shall remain the COMPANY'S SOLE and EXCLUSIVE property, and shall be PROMPTLY RETURNED upon completion of the purpose for which it was provided, or when verbally communicated, will no longer be utilized to benefit RECIPIENT, but in NO EVENT, later than TEN (10) DAYS after request by COMPANY. NO COPIES shall be made of ANY MATERIAL or DOCUMENT (S) provided under this agreement, except upon WRITTEN CONSENT of the COMPANY, ALL COPIES and samples shall likewise be RETURNED. Any attempt to disassemble or otherwise reverse engineer CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL by the RECIPIENT or the transfer of CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL to any other company or persons for any reason is strictly prohibited.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. RECIPIENT agrees that he/she WILL NOT UTILIZE ANY CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL to which he/she is exposed by reason of his/her relationship hereunder to COMPANY in such manner as to CIRCUMVENT the relationship between the COMPANY and others, nor to benefit, DIRECTLY or INDIRECTLY, from such CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL, except pursuant to any agreement between RECIPIENT and the COMPANY. 2. All CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL, which RECIPIENT shall use, view, receive or come in contact with, shall be and shall remain the COMPANY'S SOLE and EXCLUSIVE property, and shall be PROMPTLY RETURNED upon completion of the purpose for which it was provided, or when verbally communicated, will no longer be utilized to benefit RECIPIENT, but in NO EVENT, later than TEN (10) DAYS after request by COMPANY. NO COPIES shall be made of ANY MATERIAL or DOCUMENT (S) provided under this agreement, except upon WRITTEN CONSENT of the COMPANY, ALL COPIES and samples shall likewise be RETURNED.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the last sentence of paragraph 2, reverse engineering or sharing information with third parties is technically possible, which reduces the clarity of the recipient's limitations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Penal Code - § 31.05. Theft of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The law defines it to be the theft of trade secrets if one does not have the authorization to do so. By eliminating the explicit statement that no third parties can benefit from the CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL, or be provided with the CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL, the enforceability is weakened.

(f) Notwithstanding any other provision of this code, any police or other report of stolen vehicles by a political subdivision of this state shall include on the report any rental vehicles whose renters have been shown to such reporting agency to be in violation of Subsection (b)(2) and shall indicate that the renting agency has complied with the notice requirements demanding return as provided in this section. (g) It is a defense to prosecution under this section that: (1) the defendant secured the performance of the service by giving a post-dated check or similar sight order to the person performing the service; and (2) the person performing the service or any other person presented the check or sight order for payment before the date on the check or sight order. Acts 1973, 63rd Leg., p. 883, ch. 399, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1974. Amended by Acts 1977, 65th Leg., p. 1138, ch. 429, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 29, 1977; Acts 1983, 68th Leg., p. 2920, ch. 497, Sec. 4, eff. Acts 2019, 86th Leg., R.S., Ch. 1124 (H.B. 2524 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2019. Acts 2023, 88th Leg., R.S., Ch. 517 (H.B. 2897 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2023. The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see H.B. 127 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 31.05. THEFT OF TRADE SECRETS. (a) For purposes of this section: (1) "Article" means any object, material, device, or substance or any copy thereof, including a writing, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype, model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, or map. (2) "Copy" means a facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction of an article or a note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article. (3) "Representing" means describing, depicting, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (4) "Trade secret" means the whole or any part of any scientific or technical information, design, process, procedure, formula, or improvement that has value and that the owner has taken measures to prevent from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access for limited purposes. (b) A person commits an offense if, without the owner's effective consent, he knowingly: (1) steals a trade secret; (2) makes a copy of an article representing a trade secret; or (3) communicates or transmits a trade secret. (c) An offense under this section is a felony of the third degree. Acts 1973, 63rd Leg., p. 883, ch. 399, Sec. 1, eff. Jan. 1, 1974. Amended by Acts 1993, 73rd Leg., ch. 900, Sec. 1.01, eff. Sept. 1, 1994. Sec. 31.06. PRESUMPTION FOR THEFT BY CHECK OR SIMILAR SIGHT ORDER. (a) If the actor obtained property or secured performance of service by issuing or passing a check or similar sight order for the payment of money, when the issuer did not have sufficient funds in or on deposit with the bank or other drawee for the payment in full of the check or order as well as all other checks or orders then outstanding, it is prima facie evidence of the issuer's intent to deprive the owner of property under Section 31.03 (Theft) including a drawee or third-party holder in due course who negotiated the check or order or to avoid payment for service under Section 31.04 (Theft of Service) (except in the case of a postdated check or order) if: (1)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information by removing the explicit prohibition against transferring confidential material to third parties or allowing them to benefit from it. The original text stated, 'Any attempt to disassemble or otherwise reverse engineer CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL by the RECIPIENT or the transfer of CONFIDENTIAL MATERIAL to any other company or persons for any reason is strictly prohibited.' This sentence has been removed. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'theft of trade secrets' and includes 'communicates or transmits a trade secret' as an offense. By removing the prohibition on transferring information to third parties, the modified contract potentially contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets, creating a structural flaw that weakens enforceability. While the recipient is still obligated to keep the information confidential, the absence of a direct prohibition on third-party transfer weakens the company's ability to prevent unauthorized use, fitting the definition of a Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction.
